behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior across directory depth (recursive watch semantics + emitted OS events). A mocked watcher can hide real misses where nested paths never emit events on the target OS.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real daemon against a real temporary directory tree. Create `root/subdir/file.txt` after watcher startup. Assert one completed pipeline run for that nested path and output artifact present. Do not inject fake watcher events.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is cross-module state handoff (event intake -> dedup window store -> pipeline dispatch), but does not require real OS/network/subprocess to expose the core failure. Rejected System because behavior text is specifically about dedup window semantics, not filesystem event fidelity.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real in-process modules (request handler, dedup component, dispatcher) with a controllable clock or short real timer. Submit same path twice within window; assert only first dispatch executes. Submit again after window; assert second dispatch occurs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: SELF-CHECK: this test would be weaker as Unit because real handoff/state propagation between modules is the failure surface.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS duplicate event emission patterns. MOCK-MISS/MOCK-HIDE apply: synthetic single-event fakes can pass while real OS emits bursts/duplicates.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp directory. Perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename or editor-style temp swap). Capture processing count for the target file path; assert exactly one completed processing.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real OS filesystem lifecycle events (delete/recreate/inode change) and watcher reattachment behavior. In-process stubs typically miss this class of failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon continuously on a real watched path. Delete watched directory, recreate it, then add a new file. Assert daemon detects and processes new file without process restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real file metadata/timestamp resolution and arrival-event interplay. Integration fakes can enforce ideal timestamps and miss real ordering faults.
behaviors.test_description: With real filesystem and daemon running, create several files near-simultaneously with controlled creation times. Record actual processing order from daemon outputs/log hooks. Assert order matches creation timestamp ordering rule (including tie-break behavior if defined).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Rejected Integration because behavior text ties correctness to real creation timestamps at file-arrival time, which is OS/filesystem-dependent.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This race appears at real OS event timing and file write completion boundaries. Mocks usually deliver clean end-state and hide partial-write pickup bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon and begin a slow/streamed write to a large file in watched dir (keep handle open). Verify daemon does not process while writes are in progress. Close file handle/finish write, then assert exactly one processing run starts afterward.
